title: Node.js vs Browser
description: Key differences between JavaScript in Node and in the browser.

slides.title: Two Different Worlds 🌐🆚🖥️
slides.content:
# JavaScript in the Browser vs Node.js

While both use JavaScript, the environments are different:

| Browser | Node.js |
|---------|---------|
| `window` (global object) | `global` (global object) |
| Has DOM APIs (`document`, `fetch`) | No DOM – can't manipulate web pages |
| ES Modules (`<script type="module">`) | CommonJS (`require`) by default, but ES modules also supported |
| Security sandbox (can't access file system) | Can read/write files, access OS |
| UI rendering engine | No rendering, just logic |

quizData.question: In Node.js, the global object is called:
quizData.explanation: Node's global object is `global`. In browsers, it's `window`. Workers use `self`.

slides.title: What's Missing in Node? 🚫
slides.content:
## Browser‑only APIs

Node does **not** have:
* `document` or `window`
* `alert()`, `prompt()`, `confirm()`
* `fetch()` (though it's now available via a polyfill or Node 18+)
* `localStorage` or `sessionStorage`
* `XMLHttpRequest` (though you can use `http` module)

Instead, Node provides its own APIs:
* `fs` for file system
* `http` for networking
* `os` for operating system info
* `path` for file paths
* `crypto` for cryptography

slides.title: What's Shared? ✅
slides.content:
## Common JavaScript Features

Both environments share the core JavaScript language:
* Data types, operators, loops, conditionals
* ES6+ features: arrow functions, classes, promises, etc.
* `setTimeout`, `setInterval`
* `console` methods
* `Buffer`? Actually `Buffer` is Node-specific; browsers use `ArrayBuffer`.

So your JavaScript knowledge transfers – you just need to learn the Node‑specific APIs.

slides.title: ⚠️ Don't Assume Browser Objects
slides.content:
## A common pitfall

New Node developers sometimes try to use `document.getElementById()` or `window` in Node, leading to `ReferenceError`. Remember: you're on the server now – no webpage, no DOM. Use Node's modules instead.
